enrichment.short_description: ownCloud is an open-source, self-hosted file sync, share and collaboration platform built in Germany, giving organizations on-premises control over enterprise content instead of relying on public cloud storage.
enrichment.seo_title: ownCloud Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ownCloud review 2026: pricing, self-hosted file sync and share features, security, and how it compares to Nextcloud and other alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ownCloud is an open-source file sync, share and collaboration platform that organizations self-host on their own servers or private cloud infrastructure rather than trusting a third-party public cloud provider. The underlying project was started in 2010 by developer Frank Karlitschek, and ownCloud GmbH was founded in 2011 in Nuremberg, Germany, together with Markus Rex and Holger Dyroff to commercialize it for institutional customers such as universities, government agencies, and enterprises with strict data-sovereignty requirements. In 2016 Karlitschek left to start the competing fork Nextcloud, which remains ownCloud's closest and most frequently compared alternative; other competitors include Seafile, Egnyte, and general-purpose cloud storage providers like Dropbox and Box that ownCloud positions itself against on data control and compliance grounds. At the end of 2023, ownCloud merged into Kiteworks, a content-security and secure-file-transfer company, while stating its core product would remain open source under the AGPLv3 license. The product line includes ownCloud Infinite Scale (the modern server core), desktop and mobile sync clients, and a hosted consumer offering called ownCloud.online for teams and individuals who want managed storage hosted in Germany. Pricing follows a freemium/open-core model: the Community edition is free, unlimited-user, and fully open source for self-managed deployments; the commercially licensed Standard tier costs roughly EUR 5 per user per month (minimum 25 users, billed annually) and adds enterprise features plus email support; Enterprise costs roughly EUR 12 per user per month with phone support and custom branding; ownCloud.online plans start around EUR 13-15 per user per month for hosted storage. Target users are IT-security-conscious enterprises, government bodies, healthcare organizations, and universities that need GDPR-aligned, on-premises or EU-hosted file sharing rather than US-based public cloud storage. Key differentiators are its self-hosting flexibility, German/EU data residency, open-source transparency, and enterprise-grade access controls, workflow, and encryption add-ons available at the paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ownCloud is a self-hosted file sync, share, and collaboration platform that lets organizations run their own private cloud storage instead of depending on a third-party public cloud provider. It was born as an open-source project in 2010 and became a commercial company, ownCloud GmbH, based in Nuremberg, Germany, in 2011.

The platform is aimed at organizations that need full control over where their data lives, most commonly for regulatory, security, or data-sovereignty reasons. Universities, government agencies, healthcare providers, and enterprises in the EU are core users, since ownCloud can be deployed entirely on infrastructure the customer controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ownCloud provides file sync and share across desktop, web, and mobile clients, along with granular sharing permissions, versioning, and audit logging suited to compliance-driven environments.

Enterprise-tier features include end-to-end encryption, multi-factor authentication, workflow automation, custom app branding, and integrations with existing identity providers and storage backends, letting IT teams extend the platform rather than replace existing infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ownCloud's Community edition is free and fully open source with unlimited users for self-managed, on-premises deployments, including desktop and mobile apps and access to the community app ecosystem.

Paid tiers are licensed per user with an annual commitment: Standard costs around EUR 5 per user per month for a minimum of 25 users, and Enterprise costs around EUR 12 per user per month with expanded support and admin controls. A separate hosted consumer product, ownCloud.online, starts around EUR 13-15 per user per month for teams or individuals who want managed storage hosted in Germany without running their own servers.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Run ownCloud on your own servers or private cloud for full control over data location and compliance.

features.title: File sync and share
features.description: Desktop, web, and mobile clients keep files synchronized with granular sharing permissions.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Enterprise tiers support encrypted storage and multi-factor authentication for sensitive data.

features.title: Open-source core
features.description: The underlying platform is released under the AGPLv3 license, allowing code review and self-managed installs.

features.title: Workflow automation
features.description: Enterprise plans include automated workflows for document approval and content routing.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Enterprise customers can apply custom app branding for internal or client-facing deployments.

features.title: 180-day file recovery
features.description: Hosted ownCloud.online plans include extended file recovery windows for accidental deletions.

pros: Fully open-source core with a free, unlimited-user Community edition
Strong fit for organizations with strict data-sovereignty or GDPR requirements
Flexible deployment: fully self-hosted, on-premises, or EU-hosted managed option
German-based company with EU data residency for hosted plans
Enterprise features like encryption, MFA, and workflow automation at paid tiers
cons: Paid tiers require a minimum of 25 users and annual billing, which is costly for small teams
Self-hosting requires in-house IT resources to install, secure, and maintain
Interface and feature depth are often seen as less polished than fork competitor Nextcloud
Support response times are slower on lower tiers (2-hour reaction on business hours only)
Recent merger with Kiteworks has created some uncertainty about long-term product direction

faqs.question: Is ownCloud free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community edition is free and open source with unlimited users, but it requires self-hosting and self-support. Paid Standard and Enterprise tiers add commercial licensing, support, and extra features.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ownCloud and Nextcloud?
faqs.answer: Nextcloud is a fork of ownCloud created in 2016 by ownCloud's original founder, Frank Karlitschek. Both share a common codebase history but have since diverged in features, governance, and commercial offerings.

faqs.question: Can ownCloud be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting on your own servers or private cloud is ownCloud's primary deployment model, alongside a hosted ownCloud.online option for those who don't want to manage servers.

faqs.question: Who owns ownCloud now?
faqs.answer: ownCloud GmbH merged into Kiteworks, a content-security company, at the end of 2023, though ownCloud states its core product remains open source.

faqs.question: How much does ownCloud cost for a business?
faqs.answer: Commercial Standard licensing starts around EUR 5 per user per month for a minimum of 25 users billed annually, with Enterprise around EUR 12 per user per month.

faqs.question: Where is ownCloud hosted and headquartered?
faqs.answer: ownCloud GmbH is headquartered in Nuremberg, Germany, and its hosted ownCloud.online product is hosted in Germany.

faqs.question: Does ownCloud support end-to-end encryption?
faqs.answer: Yes, encryption and multi-factor authentication are available, particularly on Enterprise and ownCloud.online plans.

faqs.question: Who are ownCloud's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Nextcloud is the closest competitor, along with Seafile and enterprise content platforms like Egnyte, as well as general cloud storage providers such as Dropbox and Box.

use_cases.title: Regulatory-compliant file storage
use_cases.description: Organizations subject to GDPR or data-residency rules self-host ownCloud to keep files on infrastructure they fully control.
use_cases.audience: Government agencies, healthcare providers, and EU enterprises

use_cases.title: University and research file sharing
use_cases.description: Universities use ownCloud for interconnected private clouds that let researchers share large datasets securely across institutions.

use_cases.title: Enterprise document collaboration
use_cases.description: Enterprises replace consumer cloud storage with a self-managed alternative that integrates with existing identity and security infrastructure.
use_cases.audience: IT and security teams at mid-size to large enterprises

enrichment.short_description: Owner.com is an all-in-one restaurant technology platform providing AI-optimized websites, commission-free online ordering, branded mobile apps, and marketing automation for independent restaurants.
enrichment.seo_title: Owner.com Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Owner.com review 2026: pricing, online ordering, website and marketing features for restaurants, plus how it compares to Toast and ChowNow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Owner.com is an all-in-one restaurant growth platform that gives independent restaurants an AI-optimized website, commission-free online ordering, a branded mobile app, and automated email and SMS marketing designed to help them compete with third-party delivery apps and large restaurant chains. The company was co-founded by Adam Guild and Dean Bloembergen in 2018, originally launched under the name Placepull before rebranding to Owner.com in 2021, and is headquartered in Palo Alto, California. Owner.com has raised roughly $189 million in total funding across seed through Series C rounds, including a $33 million Series B in January 2024 and a $120 million Series C led by Meritech Capital and Headline in 2025 that pushed its valuation to about $1 billion, with estimated 2025 revenue around $81 million ARR. The platform's core pitch is helping restaurants reduce dependence on commission-heavy marketplaces such as DoorDash, Uber Eats, and Grubhub by owning the guest relationship directly: restaurants get a website built to rank in local search, direct online ordering, loyalty and rewards programs, catering order tools, chargeback protection, and AI-driven marketing campaigns, all managed from one dashboard. Pricing is subscription-based with two plans: a Flexible plan at $249 per month plus a 5% fee per order, aimed at restaurants with lower online sales volume, and a flat-rate plan at $499 per month with no per-order restaurant fee, positioned as better value once a restaurant exceeds roughly $5,000 in monthly online sales; guests are also charged a separate 5% order support fee that covers fulfillment and customer service. Both plans are month-to-month with no long-term contracts. Target customers are independent restaurants and small multi-location groups looking for a turnkey digital presence rather than stitching together separate website, ordering, and marketing vendors. Named competitors and adjacent players in the restaurant tech space include Toast, ChowNow, BentoBox, and Slice, each offering overlapping combinations of online ordering, websites, and point-of-sale integration, though Owner.com differentiates on its AI-driven marketing automation and its commission-free, subscription-first pricing model built specifically for small and mid-size independent restaurants.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Owner.com is a restaurant technology platform that bundles a website, online ordering, a branded mobile app, and marketing automation into one subscription, aimed at helping independent restaurants build a direct relationship with guests instead of relying on commission-based delivery marketplaces.

Co-founded by Adam Guild and Dean Bloembergen and headquartered in Palo Alto, California, the company rebranded from its original name Placepull to Owner.com in 2021 and has since raised over $180 million in venture funding, reaching a roughly $1 billion valuation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform centers on an AI-optimized restaurant website designed to rank in local search, paired with a commission-free online ordering system and a branded mobile app that restaurants can offer under their own name.

Marketing tools include automated email and SMS campaigns, loyalty and rewards programs, direct catering order management, and chargeback protection, all aimed at increasing repeat direct orders rather than marketplace orders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Owner.com offers two month-to-month plans with no long-term contract: a Flexible plan at $249 per month that adds a 5% restaurant fee per order, and a flat-rate plan at $499 per month with no additional per-order restaurant fee.

Guests are also charged a separate 5% order support fee on direct orders, which covers order fulfillment and customer service rather than being an added cost to the restaurant. Special rates are available for multi-location restaurant groups.

features.title: AI-optimized website
features.description: Restaurant websites built to rank in local Google search results and convert visitors into direct orders.

features.title: Commission-free online ordering
features.description: Direct ordering system that avoids the per-order commissions charged by third-party delivery marketplaces.

features.title: Branded mobile app
features.description: A restaurant-branded ordering app that helps build repeat, direct guest relationships.

features.title: AI-powered marketing
features.description: Automated email and SMS campaigns designed to bring guests back for repeat orders.

features.title: Loyalty and rewards
features.description: Built-in loyalty program tools to incentivize repeat direct ordering.

features.title: Catering order management
features.description: Direct catering order intake and management tools built into the platform.

features.title: Chargeback protection
features.description: Protection against fraudulent chargebacks on direct online orders.

pros: No commission fees on direct orders under the flat-rate plan
All core tools (website, ordering, app, marketing) bundled in one subscription
Month-to-month contracts with no long-term lock-in
AI-driven marketing automation built specifically for restaurant guest retention
Dedicated setup and migration specialist included on all plans
cons: Flexible plan still charges a 5% fee per order
Monthly subscription cost ($249-$499) is higher than pay-per-order alternatives for very low-volume restaurants
Guests pay a separate order support fee that can affect perceived pricing transparency
Newer, smaller ecosystem of integrations compared to established players like Toast
Full pricing details for multi-location discounts are not publicly listed and require sales contact

faqs.question: How much does Owner.com cost?
faqs.answer: Owner.com offers a Flexible plan at $249/month plus a 5% fee per order, and a flat-rate plan at $499/month with no per-order restaurant fee. Both are month-to-month with no long-term contract.

faqs.question: Does Owner.com charge commission on orders?
faqs.answer: The Flexible plan charges a 5% restaurant fee per order; the flat-rate $499/month plan has no per-order restaurant commission. Guests are also charged a separate 5% order support fee.

faqs.question: Who founded Owner.com?
faqs.answer: Owner.com was co-founded by Adam Guild and Dean Bloembergen in 2018, originally under the name Placepull, before rebranding to Owner.com in 2021.

faqs.question: Is Owner.com only for online ordering?
faqs.answer: No, it is an all-in-one platform that also includes a restaurant website, branded mobile app, loyalty program, and email/SMS marketing automation.

faqs.question: What is Owner.com's valuation?
faqs.answer: Following a $120 million Series C round in 2025, Owner.com reached a valuation of approximately $1 billion.

faqs.question: Who are Owner.com's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Toast, ChowNow, BentoBox, and Slice are commonly compared alternatives in the restaurant website, ordering, and marketing space.

faqs.question: Does Owner.com require a long-term contract?
faqs.answer: No, both Owner.com plans are month-to-month with no cancellation fees, and restaurants can switch plans at any time.

faqs.question: Where is Owner.com based?
faqs.answer: Owner.com is headquartered in Palo Alto, California.

use_cases.title: Reducing delivery marketplace commissions
use_cases.description: Independent restaurants move guests from commission-heavy delivery apps to direct, commission-free (or lower-fee) ordering through their own website and app.

use_cases.title: Building repeat guest relationships
use_cases.description: Restaurants use loyalty programs and automated email/SMS marketing to convert one-time diners into repeat direct customers.

use_cases.title: Launching a modern restaurant website fast
use_cases.description: Restaurants without in-house web or marketing teams get an SEO-optimized site and ordering system live quickly with a dedicated migration specialist.
use_cases.audience: Small restaurant groups and single-location owners

features: Lower upfront cost for lower-volume restaurants
Best value above ~$5,000/month in online sales

enrichment.short_description: Oyster HR is a global employment platform that acts as an Employer of Record, letting companies hire, pay, and manage employees and contractors compliantly in more than 180 countries without setting up local entities.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 2 contractors); Employer of Record from $499-$699/employee/month
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (fully distributed team)
enrichment.seo_title: Oyster HR Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Oyster HR review 2026: Employer of Record pricing, global payroll, contractor management, and how it compares to Deel and Remote.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Oyster, operating as Oyster HR, is a global employment platform built around Employer of Record (EOR) services that let companies hire full-time employees and contractors in more than 180 countries without opening local legal entities. It was founded in January 2020 by Tony Jamous and Jack Mardack, is headquartered in San Francisco, and operates as a fully distributed, remote-first company with employees spread across roughly 70+ countries. Oyster has raised approximately $291 million in venture funding, most recently a Series D round, reaching a valuation of about $1.2 billion, and it achieved B Corp certification in 2023, a differentiator it markets alongside its focus on making global hiring more accessible for companies outside major tech hubs. The platform's product suite covers EOR employment, global payroll, contractor payments and management, US PEO services, and HR advisory (People Partner Services), plus tools like a misclassification risk analyzer and visa sponsorship support. Pricing is transparent and published rather than fully quote-gated: EOR pricing starts around $699 per employee per month (dropping to roughly $499 with annual billing), global contractor management starts at $29 per contractor per month after a free tier covering up to two contractors, global payroll is around $50 per user per month, US PEO is about $114 per employee per month, and dedicated HR advisory is billed at $300 per hour; a custom Scale plan exists for high-volume hiring. Pricing excludes gross salaries, statutory employer costs like social security and pensions, and foreign-exchange conversion charges, and Oyster typically requires roughly one month of gross salary as a deposit per employee. Target customers are startups, scale-ups, and remote-first companies expanding international headcount without the cost and delay of establishing foreign legal entities. Oyster's most frequently named competitors in the EOR and global payroll space are Deel, Remote, Papaya Global, and Rippling, with Deel and Remote in particular occupying similar market positioning around fast, self-serve global hiring for distributed teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Oyster HR is a global employment platform that functions as an Employer of Record, allowing companies to hire full-time staff and contractors in over 180 countries without setting up their own local legal entities. It handles employment contracts, payroll, statutory tax filings, and benefits administration on the customer's behalf.

Founded in January 2020 by Tony Jamous and Jack Mardack, Oyster is headquartered in San Francisco but operates as a fully distributed company with no central office, reflecting the remote-work thesis the product is built around. It became a certified B Corporation in 2023.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond core EOR employment, Oyster offers global payroll for existing entities, contractor payment and compliance tools including a misclassification risk analyzer, and a US PEO product for domestic co-employment.

The platform includes an HR management layer for time off, expenses, and documentation, plus onboarding and offboarding specialists, visa sponsorship support, and access to project-based HR advisory through its People Partner Services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Oyster publishes most of its pricing rather than gating it entirely behind sales calls. Employer of Record starts around $699 per employee per month (about $499 with annual billing), global contractor management starts at $29 per contractor per month after a free tier for up to two contractors, global payroll is roughly $50 per user per month, and US PEO is about $114 per employee per month.

These fees exclude gross salaries, mandatory employer costs such as social security and pension contributions, and FX conversion charges. A custom Scale plan is available for companies with high-volume global hiring needs.

features.title: Employer of Record
features.description: Hire employees compliantly in 120+ to 180+ countries without setting up a local legal entity.

features.title: Global payroll
features.description: Run multi-country payroll in 120+ currencies for existing entities.

features.title: Contractor management
features.description: Generate compliant contracts and pay contractors internationally with built-in misclassification risk analysis.

features.title: US PEO
features.description: Co-employment services for hiring across US states without separate state registrations.

features.title: HR advisory
features.description: Project-based People Partner Services for complex HR situations and EOR support.

features.title: Benefits administration
features.description: Localized benefits packages administered as part of the EOR employment relationship.

features.title: Visa sponsorship
features.description: Support for sponsoring work visas for international hires in supported countries.

pros: Transparent, published per-employee pricing rather than fully sales-gated quotes
Broad country coverage for compliant EOR employment
B Corp certified with a stated mission around equitable global hiring
Free tier for small contractor counts
Fast onboarding (as little as 48 hours in supported countries)
cons: EOR fees are relatively high on a per-employee basis compared to some competitors
Requires an upfront deposit of roughly one month's gross salary per employee
Pricing excludes statutory employer costs and FX charges, which can add significantly to total cost
Support quality and response times vary by country according to user reviews
Custom Scale pricing for high-volume hiring is not publicly disclosed

faqs.question: What does Oyster HR do?
faqs.answer: Oyster acts as an Employer of Record, letting companies hire, pay, and manage employees and contractors in over 180 countries without opening local legal entities.

faqs.question: How much does Oyster cost?
faqs.answer: Employer of Record pricing starts around $699 per employee per month (about $499 with annual billing). Contractor management starts at $29 per contractor per month, and US PEO is about $114 per employee per month.

faqs.question: Who founded Oyster?
faqs.answer: Oyster was founded in January 2020 by Tony Jamous and Jack Mardack.

faqs.question: Is Oyster the same as Oyster HR?
faqs.answer: Yes, Oyster operates and markets its product as Oyster HR (oysterhr.com).

faqs.question: Does Oyster have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Oyster offers a free tier for managing up to two global contractors; Employer of Record and payroll products are paid.

faqs.question: Who are Oyster's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Deel, Remote, Papaya Global, and Rippling are commonly compared alternatives in the global employment and EOR space.

faqs.question: Is Oyster a fully remote company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Oyster is headquartered in San Francisco but operates as a fully distributed, remote-first team.

faqs.question: Does Oyster charge for onboarding or termination?
faqs.answer: No, Oyster states that setup, onboarding, HR advisory access, and termination processing are included in its subscription fees rather than billed separately.

use_cases.title: Hiring international full-time employees
use_cases.description: Companies use Oyster's EOR service to legally employ staff in countries where they have no registered entity, with Oyster handling contracts, payroll, and compliance.
use_cases.audience: HR and People teams at growing startups

use_cases.title: Managing global contractors at scale
use_cases.description: Distributed teams use Oyster to generate compliant contractor agreements, pay contractors in local currency, and screen for misclassification risk.

use_cases.title: US multi-state hiring without new registrations
use_cases.description: US companies use Oyster's PEO product to hire employees in states where they are not yet registered as an employer.

pricing_plans.price: Free for up to 2 contractors, then $29/contractor/month

enrichment.short_description: Pabau is an all-in-one practice management platform for clinics, medical spas, and salons, combining online booking, patient records, payments, and marketing tools in a single subscription.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (New Business plan); paid plans from GBP 50/month
enrichment.seo_title: Pabau Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pabau review 2026: pricing, booking, EMR, and marketing features for clinics and med spas, plus alternatives like Fresha and Jane.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pabau is a practice management platform built for clinics, medical spas, aesthetics practices, physiotherapy clinics, and salons, combining online booking, electronic patient/client records, forms, payments, invoicing, marketing automation, inventory, and reporting into a single system. The company was founded in 2012 by CEO William Brandham and is headquartered in Bedford, England, with additional development operations reported in locations including Skopje, North Macedonia; it now serves a reported 3,500-plus practices across more than 40 countries. Pabau differentiates itself from single-purpose scheduling tools by offering full feature parity across every pricing tier, meaning even entry-level plans get the same clinical records, forms, payments, and marketing capabilities as higher tiers; the plans instead scale by number of users and clients rather than gated features. Pricing starts with a free New Business plan for a single user with fewer than 50 clients, followed by paid tiers named Starter/Solo (single user), Team (2-3 users), Medium (4-5 users), Group (6-15 users), and Enterprise (16-plus users, multi-location support), with the published Starter tier costing around GBP 50 per month and higher tiers requiring a sales conversation. Customers can save roughly 10-20% with annual billing, and optional paid add-ons called Marketing Plus, Care Plus, and Insight Plus extend functionality further. Target users are independent practitioners and multi-location clinic groups in medical aesthetics, dermatology, physiotherapy, wellness, and general practice who need a single platform rather than separate booking, EMR, and marketing tools. Pabau's most commonly cited competitors and alternatives include Fresha, Booksy, Jane App, Practice Better, and Carepatron, each targeting overlapping segments of the salon, spa, and clinical practice management market, with Pabau positioned toward more clinically oriented practices needing structured patient records alongside scheduling and payments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pabau is a practice management platform purpose-built for clinics, medical spas, and aesthetics, physiotherapy, and wellness practices. It combines scheduling, electronic patient records, forms, payments, and marketing into one system rather than requiring separate tools for each function.

Founded in 2012 by William Brandham and headquartered in Bedford, England, Pabau now serves thousands of practices internationally, with particular strength in medical aesthetics and skin clinics that need structured clinical documentation alongside booking and billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every Pabau plan includes the full platform: online booking, calendar management, clinical/patient records, consent and intake forms, payment processing, invoicing, marketing automation, inventory tracking, and reporting.
Optional add-ons extend the base platform further: Marketing Plus adds deeper campaign tools, Care Plus focuses on patient care workflows, and Insight Plus expands reporting and analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pabau uses a per-user, per-location pricing model that scales with team size rather than restricting features by tier. A free New Business plan is available for a single user with fewer than 50 clients, and the published Starter plan costs around GBP 50 per month for one user with up to 100 clients.

Higher tiers -- Solo, Team (2-3 users), Medium (4-5 users), Group (6-15 users), and Enterprise (16-plus users, multi-location) -- require contacting sales for pricing. Annual billing saves customers roughly 10-20% compared to monthly billing.

features.title: Online booking
features.description: Client-facing booking pages with available appointment times synced to staff calendars.

features.title: Clinical records (EMR)
features.description: Structured patient and clinical record-keeping suited to medical aesthetics and healthcare practices.

features.title: Forms and consent
features.description: Digital intake and consent forms collected before or during appointments.

features.title: Payments and invoicing
features.description: Built-in payment processing and invoicing tied directly to appointments and client accounts.

features.title: Marketing automation
features.description: Automated campaigns and client communications to drive rebooking and retention.

features.title: Inventory management
features.description: Track product and consumable stock used across treatments and services.

features.title: Multi-location support
features.description: Enterprise plans support managing multiple clinic or salon locations from one account.

pros: Full feature access on every plan rather than feature-gated tiers
Purpose-built clinical records for medical aesthetics and healthcare-adjacent practices
Free entry plan for very small, new practices
Broad functionality reduces need for separate booking, EMR, and marketing tools
Serves a large international base across 40+ countries
cons: Pricing for most tiers beyond Starter is not publicly listed and requires a sales call
Per-user pricing can get expensive for larger multi-practitioner clinics
Some reviewers note a learning curve given the breadth of features
Add-ons (Marketing Plus, Care Plus, Insight Plus) carry additional undisclosed costs
Headquarters and operational location details vary across sources, making company transparency less clear than larger competitors

faqs.question: What is Pabau used for?
faqs.answer: Pabau is practice management software for clinics, medical spas, and salons, covering online booking, patient records, payments, invoicing, and marketing in one platform.

faqs.question: How much does Pabau cost?
faqs.answer: Pabau offers a free plan for very small new practices and a published Starter plan around GBP 50 per month for one user; higher tiers scale by team size and require contacting sales for pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded Pabau?
faqs.answer: Pabau was founded in 2012 by CEO William Brandham.

faqs.question: Does Pabau support multiple locations?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Enterprise plan (16-plus users) includes multi-location support.

faqs.question: Is Pabau suitable for medical practices?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pabau is widely used by medical aesthetics, cosmetic surgery, physiotherapy, dermatology, and general practice clinics that need structured clinical records.

faqs.question: What are Pabau's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Fresha, Booksy, Jane App, Practice Better, and Carepatron are commonly compared alternatives.

faqs.question: Does Pabau charge based on features or users?
faqs.answer: Pabau plans differ mainly by number of users and clients rather than by feature access, since every plan includes the full platform.

faqs.question: Can I try Pabau for free?
faqs.answer: Pabau offers a free New Business plan for a single user with fewer than 50 clients, and demos are available for paid tiers.

use_cases.title: Medical spa and aesthetics clinic management
use_cases.description: Practices use Pabau to manage bookings, structured treatment records, consent forms, and payments for cosmetic and aesthetic procedures.
use_cases.audience: Medical spa owners and aesthetic practitioners

use_cases.title: Multi-practitioner clinic coordination
use_cases.description: Clinics with several practitioners use Pabau's shared calendars and team plans to coordinate scheduling and client records across staff.

use_cases.title: New solo practice setup
use_cases.description: New, small practices use the free New Business plan to launch booking, records, and payments without upfront software cost.
use_cases.audience: Independent practitioners starting a new practice

enrichment.short_description: Pabbly Connect is a workflow automation and integration platform that connects over 2,000 apps to automate multi-step tasks without code, positioned as a lower-cost alternative to Zapier and Make.
enrichment.seo_title: Pabbly Connect Review 2026: Features, Pricing, Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pabbly Connect review 2026: pricing, lifetime deal, 2,000+ app integrations, and how it compares to Zapier and Make.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pabbly Connect is a no-code workflow automation and integration platform (an iPaaS, or integration-platform-as-a-service) that lets users build multi-step automations connecting more than 2,000 web applications, similar in category to Zapier and Make. It is a product of Pabbly, a bootstrapped software company founded in 2017 by brothers Neeraj Agarwal and Pankaj Agarwal and headquartered in Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India, operating as part of the longer-running Magnet Brains software group. Pabbly has not raised outside venture funding and has been profitable running on a small team, reportedly around a few dozen employees generating several million dollars in annual revenue. Pabbly Connect's key differentiator versus Zapier and Make is aggressive, transparent pricing: a free forever plan covers 100 tasks per month, monthly/annual subscription plans (Standard, Pro, and Ultimate) start around $16 per month for roughly 10,000 tasks with unlimited workflows and unlimited team members on every paid tier, and -- unusually for the category -- Pabbly also sells one-time lifetime-access deals (historically priced from roughly $249 up to around $699-$1,298 depending on the task tier) that eliminate recurring subscription costs entirely. The platform includes a visual, no-code workflow builder, an email parser, schedulers and delay steps, code steps supporting JavaScript and Python, an AI assistant for building workflows, and free 'internal' actions such as filters, routers, and formatters that don't consume task quota. It holds SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001:2022 certifications. Target users are small businesses, agencies, freelancers, and cost-conscious teams that need Zapier-like automation across marketing, sales, and operations tools but want to avoid Zapier's or Make's task-based subscription pricing at scale. Beyond Zapier and Make, Pabbly Connect is also frequently compared to n8n and Integromat (Make's predecessor), with its lifetime-deal pricing model in particular setting it apart from all of these subscription-only competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pabbly Connect is a no-code integration and workflow automation platform that connects more than 2,000 applications, letting users build multi-step automated workflows without writing code. It occupies the same category as Zapier and Make.

The product is built by Pabbly, a bootstrapped, founder-led software company based in Bhopal, India, founded in 2017 by Neeraj Agarwal and Pankaj Agarwal, operating without outside venture funding as part of the longer-established Magnet Brains software group.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform includes a visual workflow builder, an email parser, scheduling and delay steps, and code steps that support both JavaScript and Python for custom logic. An AI assistant helps generate workflows from natural-language descriptions.

Internal utility actions -- filters, routers, and formatters -- don't consume task quota, and every paid plan includes unlimited workflows and unlimited team member seats, which differs from competitors that often charge per seat or per workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pabbly Connect has a free forever plan covering 100 tasks per month with up to two-step workflows and no credit card required. Paid subscription plans -- Standard, Pro, and Ultimate -- start around $16 per month for roughly 10,000 tasks, with all plans including unlimited workflows and unlimited team members; longer commitments unlock deeper discounts.

Distinctively, Pabbly also offers one-time lifetime-access plans as an alternative to ongoing subscriptions, historically ranging from roughly $249 for a Standard lifetime tier up to several hundred dollars for higher task-volume Ultimate lifetime tiers, backed by a 30-day money-back guarantee.

features.description: One-time payment plans provide lifetime access without recurring subscription fees.

Rare one-time lifetime-payment option avoids recurring subscription costs
Unlimited workflows and team members included on all paid plans
SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001:2022 certified
Free internal actions (filters, routers, formatters) don't consume task quota
cons: Smaller integration ecosystem and community compared to Zapier
Interface and documentation are less polished than more established competitors
Lifetime deal availability and terms are not officially guaranteed to continue
Fewer advanced enterprise features (e.g., granular permissions) than larger iPaaS platforms
Support primarily via ticket/community forum rather than dedicated enterprise account management

faqs.question: What is Pabbly Connect used for?
faqs.answer: Pabbly Connect is a no-code automation platform for connecting apps and building multi-step workflows, similar to Zapier or Make, without writing code.

faqs.question: How much does Pabbly Connect cost?
faqs.answer: Pabbly Connect has a free plan for 100 tasks/month, with paid subscription plans starting around $16/month, plus optional one-time lifetime-access plans starting around $249.

faqs.question: Is Pabbly Connect cheaper than Zapier?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pabbly Connect is generally positioned and marketed as a lower-cost alternative to Zapier, particularly due to its lifetime-deal pricing option and unlimited team members on paid plans.

faqs.question: Who owns Pabbly?
faqs.answer: Pabbly was founded in 2017 by Neeraj Agarwal and Pankaj Agarwal and is a bootstrapped, privately held company based in Bhopal, India.

faqs.question: Does Pabbly Connect offer a lifetime deal?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pabbly offers one-time lifetime-access pricing plans as an alternative to recurring monthly or annual subscriptions.

faqs.question: How many apps does Pabbly Connect integrate with?
faqs.answer: Pabbly Connect integrates with more than 2,000 applications.

faqs.question: Does Pabbly Connect support coding for custom logic?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes code steps that support JavaScript and Python for advanced custom logic within workflows.

faqs.question: What are Pabbly Connect's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Zapier, Make (formerly Integromat), and n8n are the most commonly compared alternatives.

enrichment.short_description: Paddle is a merchant of record platform that handles payments, subscription billing, global sales tax and VAT compliance, and fraud prevention for software and SaaS businesses under one bundled transaction fee.
enrichment.seo_title: Paddle Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Paddle review 2026: merchant of record pricing, tax compliance, billing features, and how it compares to Stripe and Lemon Squeezy.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Paddle is a merchant of record (MoR) platform that lets software and SaaS companies sell digital products globally while Paddle takes on the legal responsibility for payment processing, subscription billing, global sales tax and VAT calculation, filing, and remittance, and fraud and chargeback protection. It was founded in London in August 2012 by Christian Owens and Harrison Rose, and remains headquartered in London, United Kingdom, with employees across roughly six continents. Paddle has raised approximately $293 million in venture funding across seven rounds, became a unicorn in 2022 with a valuation of about $1.4 billion, and most recently raised $25 million in a 2025 round led by CIBC Innovation Banking. Its core differentiator against traditional payment processors like Stripe is the merchant-of-record model: because Paddle is legally the seller of record, it absorbs the compliance burden of collecting and remitting sales tax/VAT in dozens of jurisdictions, which is normally a major operational cost for SaaS companies selling internationally. Pricing is a single bundled transaction fee of 5% plus $0.50 per checkout, covering billing, tax compliance, fraud protection, 24/7 customer support, and reporting with no separate monthly platform fee, migration fee, or hidden line items; Paddle contrasts this against the roughly 7%-plus effective cost businesses often pay when stitching together separate payment, tax, and billing vendors. Custom, negotiated pricing is available for businesses selling low-ticket products under $10, invoicing-based sales, or large-scale enterprise volume. Target customers are B2B and B2C SaaS companies, especially those selling internationally who want to avoid building in-house tax compliance and billing infrastructure. Paddle's most commonly cited competitors and alternatives are Stripe (particularly Stripe Tax and Stripe Billing), Lemon Squeezy (acquired by Stripe), Chargebee, and other merchant-of-record providers such as FastSpring, with Paddle differentiating primarily on its all-in-one MoR model versus Stripe's more modular, non-MoR payments infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Paddle is a merchant of record platform built for software and SaaS companies that want to outsource payments, subscription billing, and global tax compliance to a single provider. As the legal seller of record, Paddle takes on responsibility for calculating, collecting, and remitting sales tax and VAT across the jurisdictions where a company sells.

Founded in London in 2012 by Christian Owens and Harrison Rose, Paddle has grown into a venture-backed, unicorn-valued company serving SaaS businesses that sell internationally and want to avoid building in-house billing and tax infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Paddle bundles subscription billing, multi-currency payment routing, global tax registration and filing, fraud and chargeback protection, and reporting into one platform, removing the need to integrate separate vendors for each function.
As merchant of record, Paddle also manages customer-facing billing support and invoicing, reducing the operational load on a SaaS company's own support team for payment-related inquiries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Paddle charges a single, all-inclusive transaction fee of 5% plus $0.50 per checkout, with no separate monthly platform fee or migration cost. This fee covers payment processing, billing, tax compliance, and fraud protection.

Businesses selling products under $10, requiring invoicing, or operating at large scale can request custom pricing. Some users note the effective cost can run higher than the headline rate once currency conversion spreads and payment-method surcharges are factored in.

features.title: Merchant of record
features.description: Paddle acts as the legal seller, taking on tax and compliance liability on behalf of the customer.

features.title: Global tax compliance
features.description: Automatic calculation, collection, filing, and remittance of sales tax and VAT across jurisdictions.

features.title: Subscription billing
features.description: Recurring billing, plan management, and multi-currency invoicing built for SaaS pricing models.

features.title: Fraud and chargeback protection
features.description: Built-in fraud screening and chargeback defense included in the transaction fee.

features.title: Checkout localization
features.description: Localized checkout experiences supporting multiple currencies and payment methods.

features.title: Reporting and analytics
features.description: Real-time financial and revenue reporting across markets and currencies.

features.title: 24/7 customer support
features.description: Paddle handles billing-related customer support on behalf of merchants.

pros: Single bundled fee covers payments, billing, and global tax compliance
Removes the burden of registering for and filing sales tax/VAT in dozens of countries
No separate monthly platform or migration fees
Built-in fraud and chargeback protection
Well suited to SaaS companies selling internationally without in-house tax expertise
cons: 5% + $0.50 headline fee can be significantly higher than traditional payment processors for high-volume sellers
Effective cost can rise further due to currency conversion spreads and payment surcharges
Flat fixed fee component makes it costly for very low-ticket transactions
Less flexibility/control than using a non-MoR processor like Stripe directly
Custom pricing required for sub-$10 products or invoicing-based sales

faqs.question: What is Paddle used for?
faqs.answer: Paddle is a merchant of record platform used by software and SaaS companies to handle payments, subscription billing, and global sales tax/VAT compliance under one provider.

faqs.question: How much does Paddle charge?
faqs.answer: Paddle's standard pricing is 5% plus $0.50 per transaction, covering billing, tax compliance, and fraud protection with no separate monthly fee.

faqs.question: What is a merchant of record?
faqs.answer: A merchant of record is the legal seller of a transaction; as MoR, Paddle takes on responsibility for tax collection, remittance, and compliance so the software company doesn't have to.

faqs.question: Who founded Paddle?
faqs.answer: Paddle was founded in London in August 2012 by Christian Owens and Harrison Rose.

faqs.question: Is Paddle a Stripe alternative?
faqs.answer: Paddle is often compared to Stripe, but differs by acting as merchant of record and bundling tax compliance, whereas Stripe is a more modular payments platform where the seller typically remains the merchant of record.

faqs.question: Does Paddle handle VAT and sales tax?
faqs.answer: Yes, as merchant of record Paddle calculates, collects, and remits sales tax and VAT in the jurisdictions where a company sells.

faqs.question: Does Paddle charge monthly fees?
faqs.answer: No, Paddle's standard pricing has no separate monthly platform fee; the transaction fee is the primary cost.

faqs.question: Who are Paddle's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Stripe, Lemon Squeezy, Chargebee, and FastSpring are commonly cited alternatives and competitors.

use_cases.title: Global SaaS tax compliance

use_cases.description: Digital product sellers rely on Paddle's built-in fraud screening and chargeback defense to reduce losses from fraudulent transactions.

enrichment.short_description: Padloc is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted password manager for individuals and teams, offering vaults, an authenticator, and secure file storage across web, desktop, and mobile.
enrichment.seo_title: Padloc Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Padloc review 2026: open-source password manager pricing, encryption, and features, plus how it compares to Bitwarden and 1Password.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Padloc is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted password manager that lets individuals and teams securely store and share passwords, credit card details, notes, and files, with a built-in authenticator for one-time codes. The project began life as Padlock in 2014 and was substantially rebuilt and rebranded to Padloc with its version 3 launch in 2019, developed by MaKleSoft UG, a small software company based in Ansbach, Germany associated with developer Martin Kleppmann. Its core differentiator versus mainstream password managers is that its full source code -- client apps and server -- is published openly on GitHub under an open-source license and has been reviewed by three independent security audit groups, appealing to security-conscious users and organizations who want to verify the encryption implementation themselves rather than trust a closed-source vendor; Docker images are also published, enabling self-hosted deployments for teams that want to run their own Padloc server rather than use the hosted service. The apps are available across web, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android, and support family and team vault sharing. Pricing follows a freemium model: a Free tier covers basic password management, while paid tiers add storage and collaboration -- Premium at roughly $3.49/month (or ~$34.90/year) adds file storage, Family at roughly $5.95/month supports up to 5 users with shared vaults, Team and Business tiers (roughly $3.49-$6.99/month) add multi-user collaboration up to 10-20 users, and Enterprise is available at custom pricing. Target users are privacy-conscious individuals, small teams, and organizations that specifically want an auditable, open-source alternative to closed-source password managers. Padloc's most frequently cited competitors and alternatives in the password manager category are Bitwarden (also open source), 1Password, Dashlane, and KeePass/KeePassXC, with Bitwarden being the closest comparison given its similarly open, self-hostable model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Padloc is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted password manager for individuals and teams. Beyond passwords, it stores credit card details, secure notes, and files, and includes a built-in authenticator for generating one-time codes.

The project traces back to 2014 as Padlock before being substantially rebuilt and rebranded as Padloc with its version 3 release in 2019. It is developed by MaKleSoft UG, a small company based in Ansbach, Germany.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Padloc's client and server source code is fully open source and has undergone review by three independent security audit groups, letting users verify its encryption claims rather than relying on a closed-source vendor's word.

Apps are available across web, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android, with support for shared vaults across family and team plans, and Docker images are published for organizations that want to self-host their own Padloc server instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Padloc offers a free tier covering basic password management, with paid tiers adding storage and collaboration features. Premium costs roughly $3.49/month (or about $34.90 billed annually) and adds file storage; Family costs roughly $5.95/month for up to 5 users with shared vaults.
Team and Business tiers, priced roughly $3.49-$6.99/month, extend shared-vault collaboration to 10-20 users, and an Enterprise tier is available at custom pricing for larger organizations.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: All vault data is encrypted client-side before it reaches Padloc's servers.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Client and server source code is publicly available on GitHub and independently security-audited.

features.title: Built-in authenticator
features.description: Generates time-based one-time passwords (TOTP) alongside stored credentials.

features.title: Secure file storage
features.description: Store and share encrypted files alongside passwords and notes within a vault.

features.title: Team and family vaults
features.description: Shared vaults let families or teams securely share credentials with role-based access.

features.title: Self-hosting via Docker
features.description: Published Docker images allow organizations to run their own Padloc server instance.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native and web apps are available for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android.

pros: Fully open-source client and server code, independently security-audited
Self-hosting option via Docker for organizations wanting full data control
Free tier available for basic password management
Combines password management, authenticator, and encrypted file storage in one app
Cross-platform coverage across desktop, mobile, and browser
cons: Smaller company and community compared to Bitwarden or 1Password
Fewer third-party integrations and browser extension features than larger competitors
Company size and financial backing are not publicly disclosed, raising long-term-support questions for some buyers
Self-hosting requires technical setup via Docker rather than a one-click option
Less brand recognition, so fewer independent reviews and community resources exist

faqs.question: Is Padloc really open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, both the client applications and server code are published openly on GitHub under an open-source license and have been reviewed by three independent security audit groups.

faqs.question: Is Padloc free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Padloc offers a free tier for basic password management, with paid Premium, Family, Team, and Business tiers adding storage and collaboration features.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Padloc?
faqs.answer: Yes, Padloc publishes Docker images that let individuals or organizations run their own self-hosted server instance instead of using the hosted service.

faqs.question: Who develops Padloc?
faqs.answer: Padloc is developed by MaKleSoft UG, a small software company based in Ansbach, Germany.

faqs.question: How is Padloc different from Bitwarden?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source password managers with self-hosting options; Padloc additionally bundles secure file storage and an authenticator, while Bitwarden has a larger user base, community, and integration ecosystem.

faqs.question: What platforms does Padloc support?
faqs.answer: Padloc has apps for web, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android.

faqs.question: Does Padloc support team password sharing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Team and Business tiers support shared vaults for 10-20 users, and Family plans support up to 5 users.

faqs.question: How much does Padloc Premium cost?
faqs.answer: Padloc Premium costs roughly $3.49/month or about $34.90 billed annually, and adds file storage on top of the free tier.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious personal password management
use_cases.description: Individuals who want an auditable, open-source password manager use Padloc's free or Premium tier to store and generate credentials.

use_cases.title: Family credential sharing
use_cases.description: Families use Padloc's Family plan to securely share logins, notes, and files across up to 5 members.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team password management
use_cases.description: Small technical teams or organizations self-host their own Padloc server via Docker to keep full control over their password vault infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: PagerDuty is a publicly traded (NYSE: PD) digital operations and incident management platform that provides on-call scheduling, alerting, and automated incident response for IT and engineering teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 5 users); paid plans from $21/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: PagerDuty Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PagerDuty review 2026: on-call and incident management pricing, features, and how it compares to Opsgenie and Splunk On-Call.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PagerDuty is a publicly traded digital operations management platform (NYSE: PD) that provides on-call scheduling, real-time alerting, escalation policies, and automated incident response so engineering and IT operations teams can detect and resolve outages quickly. It was founded in 2009 by three Canadian engineers, Alex Solomon, Andrew Miklas, and Baskar Puvanathasan, originally in Toronto, and is now headquartered in San Francisco, California. PagerDuty completed its IPO on the New York Stock Exchange on April 11, 2019, pricing at $24 per share for an implied valuation of about $1.8 billion, and its stock jumped roughly 59% above the IPO price on its opening day of trading. The company reports that more than half of the Fortune 500 and nearly 70% of the Fortune 100 rely on it as operational infrastructure, and it employs roughly 1,100-1,300 people globally. PagerDuty's core differentiator is being the category-defining, enterprise-trusted incident management platform with more than 650-750 pre-built integrations across monitoring, chat, ITSM, and cloud tools, plus increasingly AI-driven features -- branded PagerDuty Advance -- for automated incident triage, runbook automation, and event intelligence that reduce alert noise and manual response time. Pricing follows a freemium SaaS model billed per user: a Free tier supports up to 5 users with basic on-call scheduling and unlimited API calls; Professional costs about $21/user/month billed annually (or $25 monthly) and adds SSO, external status pages, and predefined incident roles; Business costs about $41/user/month billed annually (or $49 monthly) and adds custom fields, advanced ITSM integrations, and expanded AI Actions; and Enterprise (formerly Digital Operations) is custom-priced and includes advanced automation, event intelligence, runbook automation, and dedicated customer success support. Target customers range from small engineering teams needing basic on-call alerting up to large enterprises running complex, always-on digital infrastructure. PagerDuty's most frequently named competitors include Opsgenie (Atlassian), Splunk On-Call (formerly VictorOps), xMatters, and increasingly incident-management features built into observability platforms like Datadog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PagerDuty is a digital operations management platform that provides on-call scheduling, alerting, escalation policies, and automated incident response for engineering, DevOps, and IT operations teams. It is one of the most established, enterprise-trusted names in the incident management category.

Founded in 2009 by Alex Solomon, Andrew Miklas, and Baskar Puvanathasan and now headquartered in San Francisco, PagerDuty went public on the NYSE (ticker: PD) in April 2019. It reports usage by more than half of the Fortune 500.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include on-call scheduling, escalation policies, real-time alerting via SMS, phone, chat, and push notification, and more than 650-750 pre-built integrations across monitoring, ITSM, and chat tools such as Slack and Microsoft Teams.

Higher-tier plans add AI-driven capabilities branded PagerDuty Advance for automated incident triage and event intelligence, runbook automation to execute remediation steps automatically, custom incident roles and types, and both internal and external status pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PagerDuty offers a Free plan for up to 5 users with basic on-call scheduling, unlimited API calls, and 650+ integrations. Paid plans are billed per user per month: Professional costs about $21/user/month (annual billing) and Business costs about $41/user/month (annual billing), each unlocking progressively more automation, ITSM integration, and administrative controls.

The top-tier Enterprise plan (formerly branded Digital Operations) is custom-priced and includes the deepest automation, event intelligence, and runbook automation capabilities, along with dedicated customer success support. Add-ons such as AIOps and status pages can increase total cost beyond the base per-user price.

features.title: On-call scheduling
features.description: Build and manage on-call rotations and escalation policies across teams.

features.title: Real-time alerting
features.description: Notify responders via SMS, phone call, push notification, or chat when an incident is triggered.

features.title: 650+ integrations
features.description: Pre-built integrations with monitoring, ITSM, and chat platforms like Slack, Datadog, and Jira.

features.title: Automated incident response
features.description: Runbook automation executes predefined remediation steps without manual intervention.

features.title: AI-driven incident intelligence
features.description: PagerDuty Advance AI Actions help triage and reduce noise from high alert volumes.

features.title: Status pages
features.description: Internal and external status pages communicate incident status to stakeholders and customers.

features.title: Advanced ITSM integration
features.description: Deep integrations with IT service management tools for enterprise incident workflows.

pros: Category-leading, enterprise-proven reliability with Fortune 500-scale adoption
Extensive integration ecosystem (650+ integrations)
Free tier available for very small teams
Strong automation and AI-driven incident triage on higher tiers
Public company with transparent financial reporting and long operating track record
cons: Per-user pricing gets expensive quickly for larger response teams
Advanced automation and AI features are gated behind Business/Enterprise tiers
Add-ons (AIOps, status pages, runbook automation) can push costs well beyond the base per-user rate
Enterprise pricing is not publicly disclosed and requires a sales conversation
Can be more complex to configure than lighter-weight on-call tools for small teams

faqs.question: What is PagerDuty used for?
faqs.answer: PagerDuty is used for on-call scheduling, real-time alerting, and automated incident response so engineering and IT operations teams can detect and resolve outages quickly.

faqs.question: How much does PagerDuty cost?
faqs.answer: PagerDuty offers a free plan for up to 5 users, a Professional plan at about $21/user/month, a Business plan at about $41/user/month (both billed annually), and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Is PagerDuty publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, PagerDuty trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker PD, having completed its IPO in April 2019.

faqs.question: Who founded PagerDuty?
faqs.answer: PagerDuty was founded in 2009 by Alex Solomon, Andrew Miklas, and Baskar Puvanathasan.

faqs.question: Where is PagerDuty headquartered?
faqs.answer: PagerDuty is headquartered in San Francisco, California, though it was originally founded in Toronto, Canada.

faqs.question: Does PagerDuty have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, PagerDuty's Free plan supports up to 5 users with on-call scheduling and unlimited API calls.

faqs.question: What are PagerDuty's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Opsgenie (by Atlassian), Splunk On-Call (formerly VictorOps), and xMatters are commonly cited competitors and alternatives.

faqs.question: Does PagerDuty use AI for incident management?
faqs.answer: Yes, PagerDuty offers AI-driven features branded PagerDuty Advance for automated incident triage and event intelligence, available on paid tiers.

use_cases.title: Enterprise on-call and incident management
